candidate_scores.summary: Best zero-money local-model side lane: tiny policy object, exact small-trace offline comparator, dense ratios/regret diagnostics, and side-branch OpenEvolve wrapper work. Main weakness is finite-state generalization.
candidate_scores.best_next_milestone: Run one real local OpenAI-compatible OpenEvolve iteration through Ollama or LM Studio, preserving EVOLVE-block guards and independent adapter re-evaluation.
candidate_scores.main_risk: Finite/local policy improvements may not generalize to arbitrary list size or theorem-level randomized competitive-ratio progress.

candidate_scores.summary: Strong optional parallel scaffold because current repo has exact finite oracle and fixed-shift batching infrastructure. It remains weaker than list-update as an OpenEvolve lane because the mutable object must still be fixed.
candidate_scores.best_next_milestone: Create a tiny fixed-shift batching selector or adversarial witness-generator scaffold against the existing oracle.
candidate_scores.main_risk: An exact oracle alone is not a clean evolvable object; generated artifacts may only refute toy batching selectors.

candidate_scores.summary: Best main theorem/certificate lane, with exact STT enumeration, rational certificates, and checked-in checker infrastructure. Less suitable as the first tiny local-model autonomous code-evolution target because useful mutations are proof/LP/context-heavy.
candidate_scores.best_next_milestone: Continue theorem/certificate work on source-aligned STT enumeration, LP/certificate schema, and exact small-topology artifacts rather than a tiny local-model mutation loop.
candidate_scores.main_risk: Small local models may generate plausible but invalid inequality or rounding code, and full LP/polytope paths can require heavier dependencies.

candidate_scores.summary: Excellent finite-game evaluator candidate, especially for strategy/adversary tables and LP certificates, but less directly a data-structure object-first side lane and with modern-status uncertainty.
candidate_scores.best_next_milestone: Only after list-update: define a tiny randomized-strategy table evaluator and adversary LP/checker for small n.
candidate_scores.main_risk: Exact model and modern open status remain uncertain, and the lane may become algorithmic game search rather than data-structure evolution.

candidate_scores.summary: Cheap and editable finite simulator/witness-family candidate, but model sensitivity after the ICALP 2024 progress makes finite empirical wins risky.
candidate_scores.best_next_milestone: Build a pure-Python finite witness-family generator and simulator under the exact ICALP 2024-style assumptions.
candidate_scores.main_risk: Finite load-factor simulations can overfit and may answer the wrong hashing model.

candidate_scores.summary: Good pure-Python hard-array and candidate-list stress-test target. Less compelling than list-update because small arrays can mislead about asymptotic linear-space data structures.
candidate_scores.best_next_milestone: Implement all-interval exact mode oracle plus a tiny candidate-list grammar and hard-array generator.
candidate_scores.main_risk: Correctness over small arrays does not imply cell-probe or asymptotic range-mode progress.

candidate_scores.summary: Potential-function and adversarial-trace search is theorem-relevant, but small local models are likely to confuse standard two-pass analysis with variants.
candidate_scores.best_next_milestone: After higher-priority lanes: implement a standard two-pass simulator and tiny potential-inequality checker.
candidate_scores.main_risk: Variant confusion and small-trace underfitting are severe.

primary_recommendation: Continue list_update first as the immediate zero-money/local-model OpenEvolve side lane.
secondary_recommendation: Optionally run one tiny karp_rabin_collision_detection fixed-shift selector or witness-generator scaffold in parallel, without delaying list_update.
list_update_verdict: Continue first. It wins both normal and infrastructure-discounted rankings; the win is structural, not merely due to existing infrastructure.

sunk_cost_audit.verdict: Robust. Removing the infrastructure weight leaves list_update first because of editability, cheap exact evaluation, dense feedback, dependency lightness, and crisp next milestone.

role_separation_verdict.notes: Do not make STT LP and list_update compete as if they serve the same role. STT remains theorem/certificate-first; list_update remains the cheap local-model evolution lane.

recommended_zero_money_next_step: Run the next Codex prompt included in the Markdown report: start from commit 34b18c0 on the list-update zero-money branch, attempt one real local OpenAI-compatible OpenEvolve iteration through Ollama or LM Studio, preserve EVOLVE-block guards and independent adapter re-evaluation, and record a blocker if no real endpoint is available.
warnings: Do not claim OpenEvolve has discovered a list-update policy.
Do not count the fixed-stub transport result as autonomous OpenEvolve success.
Do not assume paid OpenAI/API credits are available.
Do not claim modern open status was reverified; this audit performed no external search.
Do not treat finite list-update wins as theorem-level competitive-ratio progress without generalization evidence.
Do not demote search_trees_on_trees_lp from the main theorem/certificate lane merely because it is less suited to tiny local-model mutation.
Do not promote candidates based on exact oracles alone; require an explicit mutable object and evaluator objective.

side_lane_commits_inspected.summary: Deterministic evaluator scaffold; no OpenEvolve wrapper or policy evolution.
OpenEvolve-compatible adapter/wrapper scaffold; no evolutionary run.
Tiny run blocked by missing API credentials; no generated policy.
Real local endpoint path blocked; fixed-stub transport succeeded but was non-autonomous, non-discovery, and produced an invalid changed candidate.
